meta_title: For Students: SaaS pricing to Solve no version control (Beginner Checklist)
meta_description: Learn how to SaaS pricing and overcome no version control. A Beginner-level Checklist using Antigravity and Claude Code.

content.seo_title: For Students: SaaS Pricing to Solve No Version Control
content.seo_description: Learn to implement robust SaaS pricing while mastering version control. A beginner-friendly guide using Antigravity and Claude Code to launch your app.
content.answer_block: To solve the chaos of unversioned code, you must implement a structured SaaS pricing model that forces modular development. By using Antigravity for architectural scaffolding and Claude Code for iterative versioning, you transform messy prototypes into professional, scalable products ready for production and monetization, ensuring your portfolio stands out to employers.
content.intro:
You’re staring at a local folder filled with 'final_v2_real.py' files. The codebase is a monolith of technical debt, and you have no history of your progress—just the sinking feeling that one wrong keystroke will erase your entire project. This is the 'no version control' trap that kills student projects before they even reach a user.

But here is the secret: SaaS pricing isn't just about revenue; it’s a design constraint that forces you to build clean, modular, and versioned architecture. By treating your code like a premium product, you gain the discipline to integrate version control into your workflow, turning a chaotic mess into a professional-grade SaaS application that showcases your engineering maturity.

steps.title: Initialize Git Repository with Structure
steps.description: Before writing a single line of pricing logic, you must establish a git-tracked environment to prevent data loss.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Initialize a professional project structure including a .gitignore for environment variables and a clear directory separation for backend logic and billing services.

steps.title: Architecting the Pricing Tiers
steps.description: Define your SaaS pricing tiers as modular classes to ensure your code is testable and versionable.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Create a Python abstract base class for a SubscriptionModel and implement two concrete classes: 'Hobby' and 'Pro' with distinct feature access levels.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'create subscription_models.py with base class and two tiers'

steps.title: Integrate Versioned Commit Hooks
steps.description: Automate your version control to trigger whenever you update your pricing logic, ensuring a history of your business decisions.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Write a pre-commit hook script that validates the pricing logic tests before allowing a commit to the repository.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'setup pre-commit hook to run pytest on pricing_logic.py'

steps.title: Implement Stripe/Billing API Integration
steps.description: Connect your versioned logic to a real-world payment processor, treating the integration as a separate, trackable development branch.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Draft a secure module that handles Stripe checkout sessions, ensuring API keys are never hardcoded and logic is separated from the UI.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'integrate stripe-python library and create a checkout_session endpoint'

steps.title: Deploy and Tag Milestone Version
steps.description: Create a production-ready tag in your repository to signify your first stable release of the SaaS pricing module.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Generate a deployment checklist for a SaaS app that includes database migration tracking and environment variable validation.
steps.claude_code_command: git add . && git commit -m 'feat: implement v1 pricing' && git tag -a v1.0.0 -m 'First stable release'

pro_tips: Always use Semantic Versioning (SemVer) for your SaaS features; it signals to recruiters that you understand production workflows.
Never commit your .env file; use a template .env.example to document required variables for your peers.
Treat your 'Pricing' logic as a separate micro-service; it makes debugging billing issues infinitely easier than digging through UI code.
Use git branches for every new SaaS feature; never push directly to 'main' so you can always revert to a working version.
Document your API responses in a README file; it acts as a 'contract' that makes your project look like it was built by a senior dev.
content.next_steps: After completing this, deploy your application to a platform like Vercel or Railway and update your GitHub README with a live demo link and a clear explanation of your architecture.
content.conversion_block: You now have the technical foundation to move from a student coder to a professional SaaS builder. Don't let your code sit in a folder—take the leap and launch your first monetized app. Ready to build? Join our AI Builder community → Start your first project today.

acceptedAnswer.text: Before writing a single line of pricing logic, you must establish a git-tracked environment to prevent data loss.
Define your SaaS pricing tiers as modular classes to ensure your code is testable and versionable.
Automate your version control to trigger whenever you update your pricing logic, ensuring a history of your business decisions.
Connect your versioned logic to a real-world payment processor, treating the integration as a separate, trackable development branch.
Create a production-ready tag in your repository to signify your first stable release of the SaaS pricing module.
